title: Epistaxis recurrence review
source_review_basis: full authoritative documents inspected and archived; item-level retain/rewrite/remove/add comparisons recorded

assessment.text: Supports documenting recurrence, laterality, clinician-performed nasal examination, already completed endoscopy context, risk factors, prevention discussion, patient questions, outcome review, and follow-up without ordering procedures.

source.title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Nosebleed (Epistaxis)
source.jurisdiction: United States specialty guideline; international evidence requiring UAE emergency, ENT, haematology, procedure, and prescribing adaptation
source.population: People aged 3 years and older with current, recurrent, or prior epistaxis; selected unusual causes and special populations require additional pathways.
source.setting: Primary, urgent, emergency, outpatient, and ENT epistaxis assessment and follow-up.
source.exclusions: Exact for severity, site, recurrence, medication and bleeding-risk context, clinician examination, recurrent-bleed assessment, education, outcomes, and follow-up. It does not generate compression instructions, cautery, packing, surgery, medicine changes, or referral.

exact_location.heading: Key action statements 1–5 — prompt assessment, bleeding site, and risk factors
exact_location.locator: official fact sheet key action statements 1–5

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports documenting onset, active or recent bleeding, severity, site, anticoagulant or antiplatelet context, bleeding disorder, vital observations, and clinician concern without generating haemostasis or medicine instructions.

assessment.text: Supports documenting onset, active or recent bleeding, severity, site, anticoagulant or antiplatelet context, bleeding disorder, vital observations, and clinician concern without generating haemostasis or medicine instructions.
assessment.rationale: The exact epistaxis history section supports recurrence, duration and severity impact, associated symptoms, relevant negatives, medication and bleeding-risk context, vital signs, general appearance, and clinician concern without inferring blood loss or instability.
